content: // Suspend here indefinitely. The manager keeps emitting while this
// continuation is suspended. We resume (and the flow ends) only when
// the caller cancels the surrounding task.
// Cancellation wiring added in the next step.
_ = continuation   // placeholder. Suspension never resumes here yet.
private final class DelegateBridge: NSObject, CLLocationManagerDelegate, @unchecked Sendable {
guard let latest = locations.last else { return }
// withTaskCancellationHandler fires synchronously when the surrounding
// task is cancelled. The onCancel closure stops hardware and resumes
// the continuation so the flow body can return cleanly.
private final class DelegateBridge: NSObject, CLLocationManagerDelegate, @unchecked Sendable {
/// Called from the `onCancel` closure. Stops the manager and resumes the
/// suspended continuation so the flow body can exit.
guard let latest = locations.last else { return }

abstract.text: values through delegate methods or completion handlers rather than
the rest of your app can work with a clean, cancellable stream.
Learn FlowKit by building real features. These tutorials walk through
the patterns that come up most often: search-as-you-type, shared state
across screens, wrapping callback APIs, and writing deterministic tests.
values through delegate methods or completion handlers rather than
the rest of your app can work with a clean, cancellable stream.

content: /// A multi-subscriber location source. The hardware spins up on the first
/// subscriber and stops the moment the last one disappears, so views, view
/// models, and analytics can all observe the same stream without spawning
/// Shared location stream. Use this from every consumer.
/// The underlying cold flow. One `CLLocationManager` is created per
/// upstream activation, which `whileSubscribed` keeps to exactly one
// Request permission. The system shows the prompt once; subsequent
// calls are no-ops if permission was already granted or denied.
// Reached when the continuation is resumed by cancellation, error,
// or authorization denial. The bridge has already cleaned up.
private final class DelegateBridge: NSObject, CLLocationManagerDelegate, @unchecked Sendable {
guard isAuthorized, let latest = locations.last else { return }
private final class DelegateBridge: NSObject, CLLocationManagerDelegate, @unchecked Sendable {
/// Returns `true` when the app has sufficient location permission.
// Skip locations that arrive before the user grants permission.
guard isAuthorized, let latest = locations.last else { return }

abstract.text: Authentication state is the classic cross-cutting concern: the home
tab, the profile screen, and a UIKit sign-in controller all need to
react when the user signs in or out. Traditional approaches like singletons
a reactive stream every layer of your app can observe independently.
Search-as-you-type is a great user experience, but firing a network
request on every keystroke wastes bandwidth and hammers your server.
A debounced pipeline collapses rapid keystrokes into a single request
that fires only after the user pauses. FlowKit makes this a four-operator
Real sleeps make tests slow and flaky. FlowKit ships a
virtual clock, and a suite of
helpers that let you assert on stream values

content: // The shared flow is multicast: every consumer subscribes to the same
// CLLocationManager. The hardware spins up on the first subscriber and
// shuts down on the last unsubscribe, with no per-view-controller leaks.
// .keepingLatest() adds a one-element drop-oldest buffer between the
// GPS hardware and the view controller. When the hardware emits faster
// than the UI can render, intermediate locations are discarded and only
// the most recent update is processed.
// Update your map annotation, heading indicator, etc.
